cards.question: What are joe's member values?
cards.code:
struct Employee { int id; int age; double wage; };
Employee joe { 14, 32, 24.15 };
cards.explanation: Aggregate init assigns values in declaration order.

cards.front: What happens if you provide fewer initializers than members?
cards.back: Remaining members are value-initialized (typically zero for fundamental types).

cards.question: What are joe's values?
cards.code:
struct Employee { int id; int age; double wage; };
Employee joe { 14 };
cards.explanation: Only id is initialized. age and wage are value-initialized to 0.

cards.text: To value-initialize all members to zero: Employee joe {{c1::{ };}}} or Employee joe {{c2::{};}}}

cards.question: What are joe's values? (C++20)
cards.code:
struct Employee { int id; int age; double wage; };
Employee joe { .id = 14, .wage = 60000.0 };
cards.explanation: Designated init allows skipping members. age is value-initialized to 0.

cards.front: Can you use aggregate initialization to assign to an existing struct?
cards.back:
No. Aggregate initialization only works during object creation.
For existing objects, assign members individually or use assignment operator.

cards.front: What are the requirements for a type to be an aggregate?
cards.back:
• No private/protected members
  • No user-declared constructors
  • No base classes
  • No virtual functions
Simple structs are aggregates.

cards.explanation:
Always initialize structs:
  • Use aggregate init: { val1, val2 }
  • Prefer designated init (C++20) for clarity
  • Use empty braces {} to zero-initialize
  • Never leave structs uninitialized

cards.question: What's wrong?
cards.answer: Error: joe is const, so its members cannot be modified.
cards.explanation: Const struct = all members are const.

cards.text: Aggregate initialization assigns values in {{c1::declaration order}} of struct members.
C++20 designated initializers use {{c1::.memberName}} syntax: Point { {{c2::.x}} = 5, {{c3::.y}} = 10 }
When fewer initializers than members are provided, remaining members are {{c1::value-initialized}} (zero for fundamental types).
Aggregate initialization assigns values in {{c1::declaration order}} of struct members.
C++20 designated initializers use {{c1::.memberName}} syntax: Point { {{c2::.x}} = 5, {{c3::.y}} = 10 }
